meta.name: Ghanaian Jollof Rice
meta.subtitle: Smoky tomato-pepper rice with aromatics and optional protein

source.name: ssa
notes: Canonical Ghanaian-style jollof profile: tomato-forward, aromatic, lightly smoky, and well-seasoned.
Designed for SSA planners: supports weeknight + batch cooking + leftovers + freezer (sauce base).

yield.notes: Serves ~6 as a main with sides, or ~8–10 as a party side.

optional.label: Dutch oven / heavy pot
optional.notes: Best heat retention and even simmer for jollof.

optional.label: Oven (optional finish)
optional.notes: Optional low-oven finish for even cooking if stovetop heat is uneven.

ingredients.label: Long-grain parboiled rice
ingredients.notes: Parboiled long grain is classic; rinse quickly to reduce surface starch but keep structure.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (or a mix of neutral oil + a little palm oil)
ingredients.notes: Optional: replace 1–2 Tbsp of neutral oil with palm oil for a deeper Ghanaian profile.

ingredients.label: Tomato paste
ingredients.notes: Fry the paste to reduce raw acidity and deepen color.

ingredients.label: Blended tomato-pepper base (see note)
notes: Blend: 4 Roma tomatoes + 1 red bell pepper + 1–2 hot peppers (scotch bonnet) + 1 small onion + splash of water.
You can substitute ~1 quart tomato puree + blended peppers/onion, but the fresh blend gives the best flavor.

ingredients.label: Chicken stock (or vegetable stock)
ingredients.notes: Total liquid depends on rice brand; start with 3.5 cups and adjust if needed.

ingredients.label: Curry powder
ingredients.notes: Common in many Ghanaian home styles; keep it balanced (not dominating).

ingredients.label: Smoked paprika
ingredients.notes: Optional but helpful if you’re not getting a smoky note from the pot.

ingredients.label: Salt
ingredients.notes: Adjust to taste depending on stock saltiness.

ingredients.label: Seasoning cube (optional)
ingredients.notes: Optional, but common. If using, reduce salt slightly.

ingredients.label: Mixed vegetables (carrot/peas/green beans)
ingredients.notes: Optional Ghana-party style add-in; stir in near the end.

ingredients.label: Protein (optional): chicken, turkey, beef, or goat (pre-cooked/grilled/fried)
ingredients.notes: Jollof can be cooked without protein; serve protein on the side for cleaner rice texture.

steps.title: Rinse rice and prep aromatics
instructions: Rinse the rice briefly (2–3 quick rinses) until the water is less cloudy; drain well.
Slice onion thin. Mince garlic and grate ginger.
If making the blended base: blend tomatoes, bell pepper, hot pepper(s), and onion with a splash of water until smooth.
checks: Rice is drained well (excess water throws off liquid ratio).
Blended base is smooth (no large chunks).

steps.title: Soften onions and bloom aromatics
instructions: Heat oil in a heavy pot over medium heat.
Add sliced onions and cook until softened and lightly golden at the edges.
Stir in garlic and ginger; cook 30–60 seconds until fragrant (do not burn).

steps.title: Fry tomato paste to deepen flavor
instructions: Add tomato paste to the pot.
Fry, stirring constantly, until the paste darkens slightly and smells sweet/savory (not raw).
checks: Paste color deepens and oil starts to separate slightly.
Raw tomato paste smell is gone.

steps.title: Add blended base and reduce
instructions: Pour in the blended tomato-pepper base carefully (it may sputter).
Add thyme, curry powder, smoked paprika (if using), black pepper, bay leaves, and seasoning cube (if using).
Cook uncovered, stirring occasionally, until the sauce reduces, thickens, and the oil begins to separate on top.
checks: Oil is visible on the surface (separation is a good sign).

steps.title: Add stock and balance seasoning
instructions: Stir in stock and salt (start with about 1.5 tsp if your stock is salty).
Bring to a gentle simmer and taste the liquid: it should be slightly stronger than you want the finished rice.
Adjust salt and heat (optional extra hot pepper) now—flavors mute once rice is added.
checks: Liquid tastes properly seasoned (a bit bold).
Simmer is gentle, not violently boiling.

steps.title: Add rice and cook covered
instructions: Add drained rice and stir thoroughly to coat every grain with the sauce.
Reduce heat to low. Cover tightly (use foil under the lid if your lid vents).
Cook on low heat until rice is tender and liquid is absorbed.
checks: After 15 minutes, quickly check: surface should be steaming; if dry but rice is still hard, add 1/4 cup stock/water and re-cover.
Rice grains should be separate, not mushy.

steps.title: Steam-finish, add optional vegetables, and fluff
instructions: When rice is nearly done, optionally fold in mixed vegetables for the last 5 minutes.
Turn off heat and let rice steam (covered) 10 minutes.
Remove bay leaves, then fluff gently with a fork.
checks: Rice is tender; no wet puddles at the bottom.
Fluffing separates grains without smashing them.

steps.title: Serve
instructions: Serve hot as a one-pot meal or alongside grilled/fried chicken, turkey, beef, or goat.
Optional: serve with shito or fresh pepper sauce, and lime wedges.

fridge.notes: Cool quickly; refrigerate within 2 hours. Reheat until steaming hot.

freezer.notes: Freeze in flat portions. For best texture, freeze sauce base separately and cook rice fresh.

reheat.notes: Add a splash of water/stock and steam on low, or microwave covered with a damp paper towel.

batch.notes: Make a double batch for gatherings; jollof holds well for buffet service.

remixNotes: Turn leftovers into quick fried rice with eggs and mixed veg.
Serve with a fresh salad or sliced cucumbers to brighten.

notes: Liquid ratio varies by rice brand and pot seal. Start with the listed stock, then adjust only if needed during the first check.
For a smoky party jollof vibe, keep heat low and let the bottom lightly toast (do not burn).
If your pot runs hot, consider finishing in a low oven (300F / 150C) for 25–35 minutes after bringing to a simmer.

adapt.notes: After Step 6 begins and the pot is covered, place in a 300F / 150C oven for ~30 minutes, then steam 10 minutes and fluff.
Add vegetables at the end so the rice stays structured and not watery.
